note: This is an unofficial study aid covering common residential environmental and health hazards; complete the official Empire Learning course to earn CE credit.

modules.title: Unit 1: Indoor Air Quality & Ventilation
modules.body:
Indoor air quality (IAQ) refers to the air conditions inside a home and how they affect occupant comfort and health. Because people spend the majority of their time indoors, indoor air can have a greater impact on health than outdoor air. Common indoor pollutants include combustion byproducts (carbon monoxide, nitrogen dioxide), volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from paints, cleaners, and new furnishings, biological contaminants such as mold spores, dust mites, and pet dander, and gases like radon that enter from the soil.

Ventilation is the primary tool for diluting and removing indoor pollutants. Ventilation can be natural (open windows), spot/local (kitchen and bathroom exhaust fans vented to the outdoors), or whole-house mechanical systems that bring in fresh air. Source control — removing or sealing the pollutant source — is the most effective first step, followed by ventilation, then air cleaning.

Humidity control is closely tied to IAQ. Keeping indoor relative humidity roughly between 30% and 50% discourages mold growth, dust mites, and bacteria, while preventing the dryness that aggravates respiratory irritation. Poor IAQ can trigger asthma, allergies, headaches, and fatigue, which is why IAQ is a foundational concern in a healthy home.
summary: IAQ matters because people spend most of their time indoors, where pollutants can concentrate.
Common indoor pollutants: combustion byproducts, VOCs, biological contaminants, and radon.
Control strategy order: source control first, then ventilation, then air cleaning.
Keep relative humidity ~30–50% to discourage mold, dust mites, and bacteria.

modules.title: Unit 2: Lead & Asbestos in Older Homes
modules.body:
Lead-based paint was banned for residential use in the United States in 1978, so homes built before that date may contain it. Deteriorating paint, friction surfaces, and renovation dust are the main exposure pathways, and lead is especially harmful to young children and pregnant women. The federal Residential Lead-Based Paint Hazard Reduction Act of 1992 (commonly called "Title X") requires that sellers and landlords of most pre-1978 housing disclose known lead-based paint and hazards, provide any available records, give buyers a 10-day opportunity to test, and distribute the EPA pamphlet "Protect Your Family from Lead in Your Home."

The EPA Renovation, Repair, and Painting (RRP) Rule requires that firms performing renovation that disturbs paint in pre-1978 housing and child-occupied facilities be certified and use lead-safe work practices. This protects occupants and workers from lead-contaminated dust created during the work.

Asbestos was widely used in older building materials such as pipe and duct insulation, floor tiles, popcorn ceilings, roofing, and siding. Asbestos is hazardous when its fibers become airborne and are inhaled. The general rule is to leave intact, undisturbed asbestos-containing material alone; danger arises when the material is cut, sanded, demolished, or otherwise disturbed, releasing fibers. Suspect materials should be assessed and, if necessary, handled or removed by trained, licensed professionals.
summary: Lead-based paint was banned for residential use in 1978; pre-1978 homes may contain it.
Title X requires lead disclosure, the EPA pamphlet, and a 10-day buyer testing opportunity for pre-1978 housing.
The EPA RRP Rule requires certified firms and lead-safe practices for renovations disturbing paint in pre-1978 housing.
Leave intact asbestos undisturbed; fibers become hazardous only when the material is disturbed.

modules.title: Unit 3: Radon & Mold/Moisture
modules.body:
Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas produced by the breakdown of uranium in soil and rock. It is colorless and odorless and seeps into homes through cracks in foundations, slabs, and around pipes. Radon is the second-leading cause of lung cancer in the United States after smoking. The EPA recommends taking action to reduce radon at or above the action level of 4 picocuries per liter (4 pCi/L). Testing is inexpensive — short-term or long-term test kits, or professional measurement — and homes above the action level can be fixed with mitigation systems, most commonly sub-slab depressurization that vents soil gas above the roofline.

Mold growth is driven by moisture. Mold needs water, organic material (such as drywall, wood, or paper), and suitable temperatures; controlling moisture is therefore the key to controlling mold. Common moisture sources include plumbing leaks, roof leaks, flooding, condensation, and high indoor humidity.

Remediation basics focus on fixing the underlying water problem first, then cleaning or removing affected materials; porous materials that are heavily contaminated often must be discarded, and workers should use appropriate protection. From a transaction standpoint, known radon results and known mold or moisture problems are material facts that may need to be disclosed depending on state law, and many buyers include radon or mold testing as inspection contingencies.
summary: Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas and the second-leading cause of lung cancer.
The EPA action level is 4 pCi/L; test with kits or professionals and mitigate (e.g., sub-slab depressurization) if elevated.
Mold growth is driven by moisture; controlling water is the key to controlling mold.
Remediation fixes the moisture source first; known radon/mold issues may be disclosable material facts.

modules.title: Unit 4: Carbon Monoxide, Water Quality & Pests
modules.body:
Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colorless, odorless gas produced by the incomplete combustion of fuels. In homes it comes from furnaces, water heaters, gas ranges, fireplaces, and attached garages, and it can be deadly because occupants may not notice it. CO detectors (alarms) are the critical safeguard and are required by law in many jurisdictions, typically installed near sleeping areas; combustion appliances should also be properly vented and maintained.

Water quality is another health concern. Lead can enter drinking water from lead service lines and older lead solder, which is a particular issue in older homes; corrosion control and line replacement are common remedies. Homes served by private wells are not regulated like public water systems, so owners are responsible for periodic testing — at minimum annually for bacteria (such as coliform) and nitrates, and as needed for other local contaminants.

Pests can damage property and affect health. Integrated Pest Management (IPM) is a strategy that emphasizes prevention — sealing entry points, removing food and water sources, and reducing moisture — and uses pesticides only as a targeted last resort. Wood-destroying organisms (WDOs), most notably termites, can cause serious structural damage; many transactions include a WDO/termite inspection, and findings may need to be disclosed and remediated.
summary: Carbon monoxide comes from combustion appliances; CO detectors near sleeping areas are the key safeguard.
Lead can enter water from lead service lines and old solder, especially in older homes.
Private well owners are responsible for testing — at least annually for bacteria and nitrates.
IPM prevents pests first and uses pesticides as a last resort; termites/WDOs threaten structures and are commonly inspected.

m1.q: According to the standard control hierarchy, what is the most effective first step for improving indoor air quality?
choices: Source control — removing or sealing the pollutant
m1.explanation: Source control is the most effective first step, followed by ventilation and then air cleaning.

m1.q: What indoor relative humidity range is generally recommended to discourage mold and dust mites?
m1.explanation: Keeping relative humidity around 30–50% discourages mold, dust mites, and bacteria while avoiding excessive dryness.

m1.q: Which of the following is a common source of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in a home?
m1.explanation: VOCs are commonly released by paints, cleaning products, and off-gassing new furnishings and materials.

m1.q: An exhaust fan installed to control moisture and odors in a bathroom should be vented to where?
m1.explanation: Spot exhaust fans should be vented to the outdoors so moisture and pollutants leave the home rather than collecting in the attic or walls.

m1.q: A mechanical system that continuously brings filtered fresh outdoor air into a home is best described as:
m1.explanation: Whole-house mechanical ventilation supplies fresh outdoor air throughout the home to dilute indoor pollutants.

m1.q: Which biological contaminant commonly worsens indoor air quality and triggers allergy and asthma symptoms?
m1.explanation: Dust mites are a biological contaminant that, along with mold spores and pet dander, commonly aggravates allergies and asthma.

m2.q: In what year was lead-based paint banned for residential use in the United States?
m2.explanation: Lead-based paint was banned for residential use in 1978, so pre-1978 homes may contain it.

m2.q: Under Title X, sellers and landlords of most pre-1978 housing must provide buyers and tenants with:
choices: The EPA pamphlet "Protect Your Family from Lead in Your Home"
m2.explanation: Title X requires lead disclosure plus distribution of the EPA "Protect Your Family from Lead in Your Home" pamphlet.

m2.q: What is the general rule for intact, undisturbed asbestos-containing material?
choices: Paint over it with lead paint
m2.explanation: Intact asbestos is best left undisturbed; fibers become hazardous only when the material is cut, sanded, or demolished.

m2.q: Under Title X, buyers of most pre-1978 housing must be given how long to conduct a lead-based paint inspection or risk assessment?
m2.explanation: Title X gives buyers a 10-day opportunity to test for lead-based paint and hazards before becoming obligated under the contract.

m2.q: The EPA RRP Rule applies to renovation work in pre-1978 housing and which other category of buildings?
m2.explanation: The RRP Rule covers renovations disturbing paint in pre-1978 housing and child-occupied facilities such as certain daycares and schools.

m2.q: Asbestos fibers in older building materials pose the greatest health risk when they are:
choices: Released into the air and inhaled
m2.explanation: Asbestos becomes hazardous when fibers become airborne and are inhaled, which happens when the material is cut, sanded, or demolished.

m3.q: What is the EPA action level for radon in homes?
m3.explanation: The EPA recommends action to reduce radon at or above 4 picocuries per liter (4 pCi/L).

choices: A naturally occurring radioactive gas and the second-leading cause of lung cancer
A combustion byproduct of gas furnaces
m3.explanation: Radon is a naturally occurring radioactive gas and the second-leading cause of lung cancer after smoking.

m3.q: What is the single most important factor to control in order to prevent mold growth?
m3.explanation: Mold requires moisture to grow, so controlling water and humidity is the key to controlling mold.

m3.q: Radon enters a home primarily through which pathway?
choices: Cracks in the foundation and slab from the soil
m3.explanation: Radon seeps up from soil and rock and enters through cracks in foundations, slabs, and gaps around pipes.

m3.q: The most common radon mitigation method vents soil gas using which technique?
m3.explanation: Sub-slab depressurization draws soil gas from beneath the slab and vents it above the roofline, the most common radon mitigation approach.

m3.q: Besides moisture, mold also requires which of the following to grow?
choices: Organic material such as drywall, wood, or paper
m3.explanation: Mold needs water plus organic material like drywall, wood, or paper, so controlling moisture on those surfaces controls mold.

m4.q: Where are carbon monoxide detectors typically required to be installed?
m4.explanation: CO alarms are commonly required near sleeping areas so occupants are warned while asleep.

m4.q: Owners of homes served by a private well are generally responsible for:
choices: Nothing — the EPA tests it for them
Periodic testing, at minimum annually for bacteria and nitrates
Testing only if they smell something
m4.explanation: Private wells are not regulated like public systems, so owners should test at least annually for bacteria and nitrates.

m4.q: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) emphasizes which approach?
choices: Prevention first, with pesticides as a targeted last resort
m4.explanation: IPM prioritizes prevention — sealing entry points and removing food/water — and uses pesticides only as a last resort.

m4.q: Carbon monoxide is produced in a home by which of the following processes?
choices: Decay of organic matter in soil
m4.explanation: Carbon monoxide results from the incomplete combustion of fuels in appliances such as furnaces, water heaters, and gas ranges.

m4.q: A common remedy for lead entering drinking water through a home's plumbing is:
choices: Replacing lead service lines and using corrosion control
Boiling the water to remove the lead
m4.explanation: Lead in water is commonly addressed by replacing lead service lines and applying corrosion control, since boiling does not remove lead.

m4.q: Wood-destroying organisms most often associated with serious structural damage in a home are:
m4.explanation: Termites are the most notable wood-destroying organisms and can cause serious structural damage, so many transactions include a WDO inspection.

m1.back: Indoor Air Quality — the condition of the air inside a home and its effect on health and comfort.
Source control first, then ventilation, then air cleaning.
Roughly 30–50% relative humidity to discourage mold, dust mites, and bacteria.
Combustion byproducts, VOCs, biological contaminants (mold, dust mites, dander), and radon.
People spend most of their time indoors, so indoor pollutants can significantly affect health.

m2.front: Year lead-based paint was banned for residential use
m2.back: 1978 — homes built before then may contain lead-based paint.
Residential Lead-Based Paint Hazard Reduction Act of 1992 — requires lead disclosure for most pre-1978 housing.
"Protect Your Family from Lead in Your Home."
Renovation, Repair, and Painting Rule — certified firms must use lead-safe practices when disturbing paint in pre-1978 housing.
Leave it undisturbed; fibers become hazardous only when the material is cut, sanded, or demolished.

m3.back: A naturally occurring, colorless, odorless radioactive gas from soil; the second-leading cause of lung cancer.
Sub-slab depressurization that vents soil gas above the roofline.
Moisture — mold needs water plus organic material; control moisture to control mold.
Fix the underlying water/moisture source before cleaning or removing materials.

m4.front: Source of carbon monoxide in homes
m4.back: Incomplete combustion from furnaces, water heaters, gas ranges, fireplaces, and attached garages.
CO detectors/alarms, typically installed near sleeping areas.
Often comes from lead service lines and old lead solder, especially in older homes.
Owner's responsibility — at least annually for bacteria (coliform) and nitrates.
Integrated Pest Management prevents pests first; termites (WDOs) threaten structures and are commonly inspected.
